steps.title: Identify the structures on each side of the blank
steps.content: The blank sits after the word "Spanish" and before "The English word...". Left side: "This is why many ranching terms come from Spanish" is a complete independent clause. Right side: "The English word 'lasso' (a rope) comes from the Spanish word lazo, and 'cinch' (a belt) comes from cincho" is also a complete independent clause. Two independent clauses require a boundary punctuation mark between them.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
The rule for joining two independent clauses is to place a period (or semicolon) between them — not to leave them unseparated or use inappropriate punctuation.
  • Choice A — 'Spanish. The': places a period after "Spanish," correctly ending the first clause and starting the second as a new sentence. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Spanish (the': opens a parenthetical but never closes it within the sentence, creating a broken structure rather than a proper clause boundary. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Spanish the': omits all punctuation, fusing two independent clauses into a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Spanish? The': inserts a question mark, which signals an interrogative sentence — but neither clause is a question. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
